articles.title: On-Device Function Calling in Google AI Edge Gallery
articles.snippet: Google has introduced FunctionGemma, a specialized 270M parameter model designed to bring efficient, action-oriented AI experiences directly to mobile devices through on-device function calling. By leveraging Google AI Edge and LiteRT-LM, the model enables complex tasks—such as managing calendars, controlling device hardware, or executing specific game logic in the "Tiny Garden" demo—to be performed entirely offline with high speed and low latency. Available for testing in the Google AI Edge Gal
articles.ai_summary: Google has launched FunctionGemma, a 270M parameter model that enables efficient on-device function calling for mobile AI applications, allowing tasks to be performed offline with low latency. This development enhances user experience by enabling complex functions like calendar management and game logic execution directly on devices.
articles.why_it_matters: Enhances mobile AI capabilities by enabling offline, efficient task execution.

articles.title: Supercharge your AI agents: The New ADK Integrations Ecosystem
articles.snippet: Agent Development Kit (ADK) now supports a robust ecosystem of third-party tools and integrations. Connect your agents to GitHub, Notion, Hugging Face, and more to build capable, real-world applications.
articles.ai_summary: The Agent Development Kit (ADK) now integrates with various third-party tools, allowing developers to connect their AI agents to platforms like GitHub and Notion, facilitating the creation of real-world applications. This ecosystem aims to enhance the functionality and versatility of AI agents.
articles.why_it_matters: Expands the capabilities of AI agents, making them more versatile for developers.

articles.title: How we built the Google I/O 2026 Save the Date experience
articles.snippet: Google I/O 2026 is returning May 19-20 at Shoreline Amphitheatre in Mountain View, CA. But before the keynotes begin, you can get into the spirit of the event with our annual tradition: the save the date puzzle.  This year's experience highlights how AI can empower and accelerate
articles.ai_summary: Google I/O 2026 is scheduled for May 19-20, featuring a save the date puzzle that showcases how AI can enhance event experiences. This annual tradition builds excitement for the event while highlighting AI's role in innovation.
articles.why_it_matters: Highlights the integration of AI in enhancing user engagement at major tech events.

articles.title: You can't stream the energy: A developer's guide to Google Cloud Next '26 in Vegas
articles.snippet: While keynotes are available online, Google Cloud Next '26 in Las Vegas offers an irreplaceable in-person experience centered on networking, hands-on problem solving, and the transition to agentic AI. The event features specialized technical tracks covering everything from Gemini multimodal breakthroughs to zero-trust security on Cloud Run, providing developers with the tools to balance individual speed with organizational stability. Beyond formal sessions, the "in-person advantage" lies in over
articles.ai_summary: Google Cloud Next '26 in Las Vegas will focus on in-person networking and hands-on problem-solving, featuring sessions on agentic AI and Gemini multimodal breakthroughs. The event emphasizes the importance of direct interaction for developers in navigating complex tech landscapes.
articles.why_it_matters: Promotes the value of in-person collaboration in advancing AI and cloud technologies.

articles.title: What's new in TensorFlow 2.21
articles.snippet: Google has officially launched LiteRT, the successor to TFLite, which offers significantly faster GPU and NPU acceleration alongside seamless support for PyTorch and JAX. The update also introduces lower-precision data type support for increased efficiency and a commitment to more frequent security and dependency updates across the TensorFlow ecosystem. This transition solidifies LiteRT as Google's primary high-performance framework for deploying GenAI and advanced on-device inference.
articles.ai_summary: Google has launched LiteRT, the successor to TFLite, which offers improved GPU and NPU acceleration and supports PyTorch and JAX. This update enhances TensorFlow's efficiency and security, positioning LiteRT as the primary framework for deploying advanced AI applications.
articles.why_it_matters: Strengthens TensorFlow's position in the AI ecosystem with improved performance and compatibility.

articles.title: Introducing Wednesday Build Hour
articles.snippet: Wednesday Build Hour is a weekly, interactive "technical gym session" led by Google Cloud experts to help developers and architects sharpen their cloud skills. Moving beyond passive slide decks, the program focuses on hands-on building, covering advanced topics like AI agents, Vertex AI, and developer productivity tools. Each hour-long session is designed to provide tangible results that participants can immediately deploy into their own workflows. It serves as a consistent, dedicated space for
articles.ai_summary: Wednesday Build Hour is a new weekly session by Google Cloud aimed at helping developers enhance their cloud skills through hands-on activities. This initiative focuses on practical applications of AI and developer tools, providing immediate benefits to participants.
articles.why_it_matters: Encourages skill development in cloud technologies through interactive learning.

articles.title: Unleash Your Development Superpowers: Refining the Core Coding Experience
articles.snippet: The Gemini Code Assist team has introduced a suite of updates focused on streamlining the core coding workflow through high-velocity tools like Agent Mode with Auto Approve and Inline Diff Views. These enhancements, along with new features for precise context management and custom commands, aim to transform the AI from a general assistant into a highly tailored, seamless collaborator that adapts to your specific development style.
articles.ai_summary: The Gemini Code Assist team has rolled out updates to improve coding workflows with tools like Agent Mode and Inline Diff Views. These enhancements aim to make AI a more effective collaborator tailored to individual developer needs.
articles.why_it_matters: Transforms AI into a more personalized coding assistant, enhancing developer productivity.

articles.title: OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show
articles.snippet: TBPN, Silicon Valley's cult-favorite tech podcast, will operate independently, even as it's overseen by chief political operative Chris Lehane.
articles.ai_summary: OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a popular tech podcast, which will continue to operate independently under the oversight of Chris Lehane. This acquisition reflects OpenAI's interest in expanding its media presence and influence in the tech community.
articles.why_it_matters: Signals OpenAI's strategy to broaden its reach and engagement in the tech landscape.

articles.title: PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default
articles.snippet: If you use the AI-powered note-taking app Granola, you might want to double-check your privacy settings. Though Granola says your notes are "private by default," it makes them viewable to anyone with a link, and also uses them for internal AI training unless you opt out. Granola describes itself as an "AI notepad for people […]
articles.ai_summary: Users of the AI note-taking app Granola should review their privacy settings, as notes are viewable by anyone with a link by default, and are used for internal AI training unless opted out. This raises concerns about user privacy and data security.
articles.why_it_matters: Highlights critical privacy issues in AI applications that users must be aware of.

articles.title: New Rowhammer attacks give complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs
articles.snippet: Both GDDRHammer and GeForge hammer GPU memory in ways that compromise the CPU.
articles.ai_summary: New Rowhammer attacks, GDDRHammer and GeForge, exploit vulnerabilities in Nvidia GPUs, allowing attackers to gain complete control over affected machines. This development poses significant security risks for systems utilizing these GPUs.
articles.why_it_matters: Raises alarms about security vulnerabilities in widely used hardware, impacting many users.

articles.title: It’s not easy to get depression-detecting AI through the FDA
articles.snippet: For the past seven years, the California-based startup Kintsugi has been developing AI designed to detect signs of depression and anxiety from a person's speech. But after failing to secure FDA clearance in time, the company is shutting down and releasing most of its technology as open-source. Some elements may even find a second life […]
articles.ai_summary: Kintsugi, a startup focused on AI for detecting depression and anxiety, is shutting down after failing to secure FDA clearance. The company plans to release most of its technology as open-source, potentially benefiting future developments.
articles.why_it_matters: Highlights challenges in getting health-related AI technologies approved.

articles.title: Microsoft’s new ‘superintelligence’ game plan is all about business
articles.snippet: Mustafa Suleyman has been preparing for his new job description for a long time. Suleyman is Microsoft's inaugural CEO of AI, but after the company underwent a large-scale restructuring in mid-March, he's handed off some duties and shifted focus to chasing superintelligence. Though the news was only made public last month, he tells The Verge, […]
articles.ai_summary: Microsoft's Mustafa Suleyman is focusing on developing 'superintelligence' as part of a restructuring effort. This strategic shift aims to position Microsoft as a leader in advanced AI solutions.
articles.why_it_matters: Indicates a significant pivot towards ambitious AI goals at Microsoft.
